interactions.summary: Jiang extends the truth/consequence answer through education work: he entered study-abroad counseling to help Chinese students, found little demand for goodness, reached rock bottom, and chose not to sell recommendation-letter access even when it could make him rich because truth and goodness were the life he wanted to live.

Jay clarifies the Afghanistan example, asking whether the boss wanted Jiang to lie or whether there really were cases; Jiang says there were a couple of cases but malnutrition made chicken-killing the morally absurd focus.

Jay pushes the analogy back to public speech, saying Jiang thought the truth could do damage if expressed badly, then compares it with his own post-9/11 concern about how to tell truths about Islam, Judaism, Zionism, and violence without triggering backlash against innocents.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_00 is Jiang through seg-0118 answers; SPEAKER_01 is Jay in the clarifying questions and extended analogy. Jay's analogy continues into packet 16.

claims.claim: Jiang says he rejected a lucrative corrupt path in Chinese study-abroad admissions because he wanted to promote goodness rather than sell elite access.
Jiang clarifies that Afghanistan had a couple of avian flu cases, but he thought malnutrition made the institutional response of killing chickens morally misguided.
claims.temporal_scope: Clarification of past work experience, stated 2026-04-01.

claims.claim: Jay frames his own public work as myth-making with truth rather than journalism, and says post-9/11 language about Islam made him worry about both disingenuous denial and backlash against innocent Muslims.
claims.temporal_scope: Jay's self-description and post-9/11 frame on 2026-04-01.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang's temptation is almost comic in scale: one Yale recommendation-letter hustle could be a million dollars, and he refuses because it would betray the life he claims to live.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It makes truth/goodness biographical rather than abstract.

signature_moments.moment: Jay calls himself a myth maker who uses truth, distinguishing documentary practice from neutral journalism.
signature_moments.source_phrase: a myth maker that uses the truth
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This gives Jay's critique unusual symmetry with Jiang: both know public truth arrives through story.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's moral refusal to turn education credentials into corrupt admissions brokerage.
Jay's description of documentary work as shaping true material into public myth rather than pure journalism.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang says meeting his wife about ten years before the 2026 interview changed his life during a period of unemployment and rock bottom.
